points.text: Collect time‑series metrics: cache hit/miss, DB query count, latency, CPU, and request volume per endpoint.
Compare metrics before and after the feature release to pinpoint which endpoints saw the biggest hit‑rate drop.
Inspect how the new query parameters are incorporated into cache keys; ensure they don’t create high‑cardinality keys.
Verify that cache TTL and eviction policy haven’t changed, causing premature evictions.
Profile the application to locate CPU hotspots introduced by extra parameter handling or serialization.
Run EXPLAIN on the affected SQL queries to detect missing indexes or full table scans caused by new filters.
Add request‑level tracing to correlate specific parameter combos with cache misses and DB reads.
If high cardinality is unavoidable, consider secondary indexes in cache (e.g., hash of parameters) or query result caching.
Implement back‑off or rate‑limiting for hot paths while you refactor, to reduce DB pressure.
After changes, monitor hit‑rate, DB reads, and CPU to confirm load reduction; iterate as needed.
Collect time‑series metrics for cache hit/miss, DB query count, and CPU usage per endpoint.
Correlate new query parameters with cache key generation; verify key granularity isn’t causing fragmentation.
Enable detailed tracing to see which service calls bypass cache and hit the DB after the change.
Profile the code paths handling the new parameters to identify CPU hotspots and expensive serialization.
Run a controlled A/B test: revert to old parameter set and measure cache hit rate and DB load.
If cache key space exploded, consider normalizing parameters or using a composite key with hashing.
Evaluate batching of DB reads for high‑frequency queries; combine multiple requests into a single query.
Adjust timeout budgets and circuit‑breaker thresholds to prevent cascading overload while fixing root cause.
Collect baseline metrics: pg_stat_database cache hit, pg_stat_bgwriter, and OS vmstat to confirm cache drop and I/O increase.
Enable pg_stat_statements and identify queries with the largest increase in execution count or total time since the change.
For each hot query, run EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) with current parameters to see plan changes and buffer usage.
Check if added query parameters cause planner misestimation (low selectivity) leading to sequential scans.
Verify that indexes used before still match the new WHERE clauses; create or adjust multicolumn indexes if needed.
Test parameter sniffing by forcing generic plans (plan_cache_mode = force_generic_plan) to see if prepared statements cause suboptimal plans.
Compare actual vs. estimated rows; if mismatch is high, update statistics with ANALYZE or increase default_statistics_target.
Monitor work_mem and maintenance_work_mem; insufficient memory can force disk‑based sorts, raising CPU and I/O.
If cache miss persists, consider increasing shared_buffers or using pg_prewarm to warm frequently accessed tables.
Iteratively apply the most impactful change, re‑measure metrics, and repeat until cache hit and read rates return to acceptable levels.
Gather baseline metrics: cache hit rate, DB read QPS, CPU, latency per endpoint before and after change.
Enable query tracing or slow‑query log to list statements whose frequency or execution time grew.
Compare execution plans pre‑ and post‑feature; look for full table scans or missing index usage caused by new parameters.
Check cache key design; added parameters may break hit patterns—adjust keys or add result‑set caching for the new queries.
Create or tune indexes that cover the new filter predicates; consider covering indexes to avoid extra lookups.
If reads stay high, evaluate read‑replica scaling or sharding to distribute load while keeping consistency guarantees.
Collect time‑series of cache hit/miss, DB read count, and CPU usage per endpoint to spot correlation.
Map new query parameters to cache keys; assess if key cardinality exploded causing more misses.
Profile request handling to locate CPU hotspots in query construction or serialization.
Check cache TTL and eviction policy; ensure cache size can accommodate the larger key space.
Run an A/B test disabling the new parameters to quantify their impact on hit rate and DB load.
Add cache warm‑up or secondary index for high‑cardinality queries to improve hit probability.
Introduce read‑through caching or materialized views to offload frequent DB reads.
Tune autoscaling thresholds and SLO budgets based on the observed load increase.
